content.full_text:
City of Fremont

City Attorney's Office
39700 Civic Center Drive
P.O. Box 5006
Fremont, California 94537
(415) 745-2730

February 21, 1990

Ms. Blanca Breeze
Staff Attorney
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95807

Dear Blanca:

You will recall from our telephone conversation earlier this
month that the City of Fremont has some concerns regarding
application of the nexus provisions of Section 1B702.1 of the
FPPC regulations as they may pertain to a city councilmember who
is employed as a real property appraiser. I do not presently
have actual facts on which to request a formal FPPC opinion
letter. However, it seems reasonably certain that there will
arise in the near future factual circumstances similar to those I
will set out below as hypotheticals. I think securing the advice
of your agency on these hypotheticals will be helpful in
preparing to promptly address anticipated actual problems as they
arise.

I will begin my inquiry by stating that my basic
understanding of the nexus disqualifications is that it pertains
only to sources of income and not to other categories of
financial interests which the regulations specifically address.
Further, it is my understanding that direct involvement of a
nexus source of income is not required to bring into application
Section 1B702.1's conclusive presumption that a decision will
have an effect which will be material as to the nexus source of
income. It therefore seems that, if an official has received
income of $250 or more in the prior 12 months from any before
appearing entity which has somehow sought to achieve a goal or
purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided or hindered by
the decision, the effect of the decision will be material as to
that source of income entity, and the official will always be
disqualified even if the source of nexus income is not directly
involved in and will not be affected by the decision. Because
the "no-financial-effect" exception provided by subdivision
(c)(2) applies only to persons or entities who appear, it seems
that a conclusive presumption of materiality must always apply as
to any non-appearing nexus sources of income. Assuming my above-
stated understanding of Section 1B702.1 is correct, I still have
unanswered concerns which I hope your agency can address.

Blanca Breeze
February 21, 1990
Page Two


     First, if the decision will have absolutely no financial
effect on the official's not-directly-involved nexus source of
income, why is there still a disqualification? I make this
inquiry because as observed above, the exception provided for by
subdivision (c)(2) does not apply to sources of income who do not
appear before the officialli and it therefore seems that a literal
reading of all of Section 1870Z.1 would require a dis-
qualification in such a case.  However, this result does not seem
to further the purposes of the PRA.  For a hypothetical
illustration of my concern, please assume a self-employed
councilmember appraiser who has done an appraisal for the overall
project designer for a developer who comes before the city
council with a complete project proposal based in part on the
councilmember appraiser's work.  The council's decision will have
absolutely no financial effect on either the overall project
designer or on the council member appraiser because all the plans
for the project are complete, and approval or disapproval of the
project will clearly not require any more appraisal or design
work.  Is the councilmember appraiser disqualified under Section
1870Z.1? If he is disqualified, how can this result be justified
as consistent with the legislation on which the regulations must
be based?

     For a second question, please assume the councilmember is a
self-employed appraiser who was retained by the City prior to
being elected to the city council to appraise a site for
acquisition for a park.  There is clearly a nexus between the
councilmember's receipt of income and the municipal purpose of
developing a park.  Also, the City is clearly a source of income
to the councilmember appraiser.  Will the councilmember appraiser
be disqualified from participating in a decision to buy land
from a willing seller at the appraised price? If so, will the
disqualification be brought about by the nexus or, alternatively,
by the appearing source of income provisions of Section 1870Z.1
with the City itself being the appearing source? I would be
concerned that disqualification on any basis would not seem to
comport with the overall purpose of the PRA's disqualification
provisions.  That is, since the councilmember appraiser has
already received all the income he will ever receive in
connection with the appraisal, how would the PRA's purposes be
furthered by his disqualification in this instance? Does the PRA
require that a councilmember be prohibited from participating in
a land purchase decision simply because he has been paid by the
City for an appraisal of the land in question (a nexus
disqualification?) or because the decision will have a financial
effect on a source of income entity which happens to be the very
City for which he helps own? In considering whether the appearing
source of income disqualification applies, I ask that you
consider that a salary or per diem paid to the councilmember
appraiser by the City would not be disqualifying.  See Section
B2030(b)(2).

Bianca Breeze
February 21, 1990
Page Three


     For a another disqualification question, please assume the
same hypotheticals set out in the first and second inquiries but
change the councilmember appraiser's employment status to that of
an employee of a two-appraiser, family-operated appraisal firm.
Under either of the prior hypotheticals, would there be a
disqualification if the councilmember were paid a regular salary?
What if he receives no regular salary and is instead paid either
an hourly rate on a job-by-job basis or a certain percentage of
each appraisal fee his employing firm earns for his appraisals.
It seems that changing the status of the councilmember appraiser
from self-employed to employed by a firm may require a different
result because the source of income will now be the employing
firm rather than the project designer as in the first
hypothetical or the city as in the second. Also it might be
asserted that the involvement of an employee appraiser is far
enough removed from the subject matter of the decisions that the
test of subsection (d) of Section 1870E.1 would not be met. In
this context, I ask that you inform me whether there has been
established a test of how remote a nexus can be and still trigger
a disqualification. For example, it does not seem reasonable
that a disqualification should occur if an official had merely
appraised an office building for an architect purchaser who
intended to use and did use the building as the business location
where he designed a project later brought before the appraiser
official by a developer who retained and fully paid the architect
for a one-shot project design.

     One remaining area of inquiry pertains to what constitutes a
source of income for a non-salaried appraiser whose earnings are
either an hourly rate on a job-by-job basis or a percentage of
the fees charged by his employing appraisal firm for the
appraisals done by him. A literal reading of the law would lead
one to conclude that the appraiser's only source of income is his
employing appraisal firm. Is this a safe and fair conclusion, or
should there be consideration as to whether the following might
also constitute sources of income?

     •    Persons who order appraisals from the firm
          solely for their own interest.

     •    Professionals (such as attorneys) who order
          appraisals from the firm in their clients'
          interest.

     •    Clients on whose behalf appraisals
          are ordered from the firm--e.g., persons
          represented by attorneys who secure
          appraisals to assist in serving their
          clients.

Bianca Breeze
February 21, 1990
Page Four


     It seems that responding to all of the above inquires may
require a great deal of effort.  Please be assured that there are
substantial reasons for making these inquiries and that it is
very likely that factual circumstances closely resembling those
set out in the hypotheticals will arise in the near future.

     I thank you for your anticipated courtesy and cooperation.

                                          Sincerely,

                                          [signature]
                                          LYLE L. LOPUS
                                          Assistant City Attorney

LLL:inf

cc:  Kurt Roessler
